description: Canonical cross-binding nRPC compatibility fixture for the client-streaming + duplex shapes shipped under NET_RPC_ABI_VERSION = 0x0002 (NRPC_BINDINGS_PLAN.md Phase 2). Two services, each with a documented contract and a fixed set of ok_cases. Every binding's wire-format compat test loads this file and asserts: encoding the request stream on each side, dispatching through the canonical handler, and decoding the response stream yields outputs that semantically match the fixture. Status codes, header names, and the JSON shapes are all part of the contract.

canonical_flag_end_placement.rule: FLAG_RPC_REQUEST_END is carried by the LAST chunk that contains user data, not a trailing empty-body terminator. Bindings emitting `request_items = [a, b, c]` MUST set FLAG_END on the chunk carrying `c` (not on a fourth empty chunk). For the 1-item case the FLAG_END rides on the ONE chunk that carries that item. For the empty-items case, the initial REQUEST itself carries FLAG_RPC_CLIENT_STREAMING_REQUEST | FLAG_RPC_REQUEST_END with an empty body (the 'pure terminator' fast path).
canonical_flag_end_placement.rationale: Trailing-empty-FLAG_END was tolerated by the Rust substrate's terminator-semantics rule (empty body + FLAG_END = pure terminator, suppressed from the handler's stream) but it costs an extra event per call. Pinning the data-bearing-FLAG_END as canonical keeps all bindings byte-identical on the wire and avoids the substrate's 'is_pure_terminator' branch.

examples.zero_items: REQUEST(flags = CLIENT_STREAMING_REQUEST | REQUEST_END, body = []). Zero REQUEST_CHUNKs.
examples.one_item: REQUEST(flags = CLIENT_STREAMING_REQUEST | REQUEST_END, body = encode(item_0)). Zero REQUEST_CHUNKs.
examples.n_items: REQUEST(flags = CLIENT_STREAMING_REQUEST, body = encode(item_0)). REQUEST_CHUNK(flags = 0, body = encode(item_1)) ... REQUEST_CHUNK(flags = REQUEST_END, body = encode(item_{N-1})).

error_cases.description: Cross-binding-pinned error paths. Bindings MUST surface the codes/status documented here for the given trigger; the cross_lang_streaming integration test exercises each via the same direct-fold-dispatch pattern as the ok_cases.

cases.name: client_stream_decode_failure
cases.trigger: Caller emits a REQUEST_CHUNK whose body is not valid JSON for the request_item_shape (e.g., the raw bytes `not-json-shape`).
cases.diagnostic_substring: typed streaming handler: bad request body

cases.name: client_stream_handler_application_error
cases.trigger: Server-side handler returns Err(String) (typed) or raises the typed application exception (Node/Python).

cases.name: client_stream_cancel_mid_send
cases.trigger: Caller drops the call handle after sending at least one chunk and before finish().

cases.name: duplex_cancel_from_caller
cases.trigger: Caller drops BOTH halves (or the combined DuplexCall) before clean close.
cases.diagnostic_substring: server observed CANCEL during duplex handler execution

cases.name: duplex_decode_failure
cases.trigger: Server emits a RESPONSE chunk whose body is not valid JSON for response_shape.

services.description: Drain a stream of {text, numbers} request items. Return a single response: { echo: concatenation of all text fields joined by ' ', total: sum of all numbers across all items, count: number of items consumed }.

response_shape.total: i64 — sum of all numbers across items

services.description: Drain a stream of {text, numbers} request items. Emit one response per request item: { echo: item.text, sum: sum of item.numbers }. After processing all items, emit one final summary response: { echo: 'summary', sum: count_of_items }. Handler returns Ok after EOF on the request stream.

response_shape.echo: str — item's text, or 'summary' for the final frame
response_shape.sum: i64 — item's numbers summed, or count_of_items for the summary
